claims.claim:
Kay's tomb at Giza was discovered during an excavation that revealed layers of debris ranging in thickness from 5-10 cm.  
   The western wall of Kay's tomb at Giza exhibited significant damage to its painted decoration due to detachment from the adobe surface.  
   Conservation efforts included stabilizing the wall with Paraloid B72 and treating loose plaster fragments on-site.

claims.doc_summary: The document focuses on the conservation efforts for Kay's tomb at Giza, detailing the discovery of its layers of debris, the damage to its painted decorations, and the materials used for stabilization. It highlights the use of Paraloid B72 and ethyl silicate as effective consolidants, along with laboratory testing to determine suitable treatments for weathered surfaces. The document also describes challenges faced during excavation, such as adhering debris and salt crystallization, and the construction of a temporary wooden roof to protect the site.

claims.claim:
The preparatory layers of Kay's tomb at Giza were analyzed and found to contain quartz (46-1045), calcite (05-0586), gypsum (33-0311), and dolomite (11-0078).  
   These materials were identified in the clay plaster used for the mural paintings on the walls of Kay's tomb at Giza.  
   The ratio of calcite to gypsum was nearly 1:1, indicating a balanced composition in the preparatory layers.

claims.claim:
Testing was conducted on laboratory specimens mimicking the mural paintings of Kay's tomb at Giza to simulate weathering effects.  
   Various polymers and emulsions were tested for their suitability as consolidants, including polyvinyl acetate, ethyl silicate, and primal AC33.  
   Primal AC33 was found to be the most effective emulsion for adhering loose plaster fragments on vertical walls.

claims.claim:
Ethyl silicate was identified as a suitable consolidant for the adobe surfaces of Kay's tomb at Giza due to its ability to react with free hydroxyl groups (-OH) in the clay particles.  
   This method has been successfully used over the past twenty years for earthen structures containing similar materials.  
   Its polymerization helps prevent material detachment during treatment by penetrating and coating loosened particles.

claims.claim:
The excavations of the Sphinx (entry 144) were conducted between 1937 and 1938 under the supervision of Abdel Mohsen Bakir and Fawzi Ibrahim, focusing on the area around the iconic statue.  
   These excavations aimed to uncover hidden structures and artifacts near the Sphinx, including rock-cut tombs and mud-brick temples.  
   The findings were published in 1953 and 1960, providing significant insights into ancient Egyptian funerary practices.

claims.claim:
Khufu's boat was discovered in a rectangular pit near the Great Pyramid of Giza.  
   The boat measures 43.6 meters in length and is constructed from Lebanese cedar planks.  
   It was carefully assembled using carpenter's marks to ensure precise fitting of its components.
claims.title: Abubakr Fs Ricke
claims.author: Unknown
claims.doc_summary: The document details the construction methods and materials used to build Khufu's boat, discovered near the Great Pyramid of Giza. It highlights advanced woodworking techniques, including the use of Lebanese cedar planks, copper alloy nails, and carpenter's marks for precise assembly. The findings reveal a highly organized and methodical approach to building this ancient vessel, showcasing the technical expertise of early Egyptian civilization.

claims.claim:
The hull of Khufu's boat was divided into four sections, each marked with distinct symbols.  
   These sections were labeled with hieroglyphs: the northeastern section used the sign <=<, the southeastern section used four strokes |||, the northwestern section used a cross +, and the southwestern section used the sign .

claims.claim:
Ni-htp-hnmw's mastaba at Giza has dimensions of 180 cm x 160 cm x height about 375 cm.  
   This structure is classified as a "core type VI" according to Reisner's classification, with a rubble core of nummulitic stone encased in crude brick retaining walls.  
   The southern end of the eastern face features a small enclosure protruding from the main plan, connected by a door aligned with the axis of the main false-door.
claims.title: Abu Bakr Giza

claims.doc_summary: The document details the construction and inscriptions of two mastabas at Giza, Ni-htp-hnmw and Ihy-htpw. Both structures are classified as "core type VI," featuring nummulitic stone cores with crude brick retaining walls. Their false-doors and offering-tables are described, along with inscriptions identifying the individuals as "Royal acquaintance."

claims.claim:
The small enclosure at Ni-htp-hnmw's mastaba has dimensions of 290 cm x 220 cm and a height of about 100 cm.  
   This enclosure is constructed from crude brick, similar to the mastaba itself, and its entrance door measures 70 cm in width and 75 cm in depth.  
   The doorway connecting it to the corridor is surmounted by a drum inscribed with hieroglyphs identifying Ni-htp-hnmw as "Royal acquaintance, regent of Hivt-hwjw."

claims.doc_summary: The document details the construction and inscriptions of two mastabas at Giza, Ni-htp-hnmw and Ihy-htpw. Both structures are classified as "core type VI," featuring nummulitic stone cores with crude brick retaining walls. Their false-doors and offering-tables are described, along with inscriptions identifying the individuals as "Royal acquaintance."

claims.claim:
The inscriptions on the false-doors of both mastabas at Giza provide specific names and titles, such as "Royal acquaintance" and "regent of Hivt-hwjw," offering insights into the social status of the individuals buried there.  
    These hieroglyphic texts are crucial for identifying the owners of the tombs and understanding their roles in society.

claims.claim:
The Alford sphinx (R.M.S. 1984.405) is a small calcite statuette measuring approximately 13 cm in height.  
   It depicts the king in the form of a sphinx kneeling to make an offering, specifically holding nw-pots as libations.  
   This iconography is unique among known sphinx statuettes and suggests a votive or propitiatory function.
claims.title: Aldred Fs Edwards
claims.author: Unknown
claims.doc_summary: The Alford sphinx is a rare calcite statuette from the Old Kingdom period, depicting the king as a sphinx offering libations in nw-pots. It was likely dedicated to Rec-Herakhte and reflects early Egyptian votive practices, with stylistic parallels to other sphinxes like the Brooklyn Museum's example attributed to Phiops I. This unique artifact highlights the sphinx's role as a royal representative and its connection to solar cults at Heliopolis.

claims.claim:
The Alford sphinx was likely dedicated to Rec-Herakhte, the chief deity of Heliopolis, as indicated by its dedicatory inscription.  
   It is stylistically comparable to other early sphinx statuettes, such as the one in the Brooklyn Museum attributed to Phiops I.  
   This comparison suggests a close dating for both pieces within the Old Kingdom period.

claims.claim:
The Alford sphinx was originally part of a larger votive ensemble or model temple, as suggested by its diminutive size and design.  
   It may have been part of a set of sphinxes placed at key points in a temple's architecture, such as doorways or altars.  
   This interpretation aligns with the reconstruction of the Gorringe example dedicated by Sethos I in The Brooklyn Museum.

claims.claim:
The Alford sphinx is one of only two known examples of a sphinx statuette presenting libations from nw-pots, the other being the faience sphinx of Amenophis III.

claims.claim: Joseph Lindon Smith was awarded an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters degree by the University of New Hampshire on June 13, 1948, in recognition of his contributions to art and archaeology. The presentation was made at Durham, New Hampshire, during a ceremony where he was described as a teacher, artist, author, and international traveler.
claims.title: Allam Smith
claims.author: Unknown
claims.doc_label: Joseph Lindon Smith Honorary Degree Award
claims.doc_summary: Joseph Lindon Smith was awarded an honorary Doctor of Humane Letters by the University of New Hampshire in 1948 for his contributions to art and archaeology. Known as a teacher, artist, author, and international traveler, Smith's work included reproducing historical murals with meticulous detail and teaching Egyptian painters in Egypt. His home, "Loon Point" in Dublin, New Hampshire, was celebrated as a museum of fine arts, reflecting his artistic legacy and dedication to community affairs.

claims.claim: Smith's painting technique allowed him to reproduce both wood and stone with remarkable facility, as demonstrated in his work at "Loon Point," the family home in Dublin, New Hampshire. The house is situated on a peninsula extending north into one of the most beautiful sections of Dublin Lake.
